tags.name: norm:sip_sie
tags.text: The sip register is an SXLEN-bit read/write register containing information on pending interrupts, while sie is the corresponding SXLEN-bit read/write register containing interrupt enable bits. Interrupt cause number i (as reported in CSR scause, ) corresponds with bit i in both sip and sie. Bits 15:0 are allocated to standard interrupt causes only, while bits 16 and above are designated for platform use.

tags.name: norm:sie_sip_supervisor_strap
tags.text: An interrupt i will trap to S-mode if both of the following are true: (a) either the current privilege mode is S and the SIE bit in the sstatus register is set, or the current privilege mode has less privilege than S-mode; and (b) bit i is set in both sip and sie.

tags.name: norm:sie_sip_strap_time_constraint
tags.text: These conditions for an interrupt trap to occur must be evaluated in a bounded amount of time from when an interrupt becomes, or ceases to be, pending in sip, and must also be evaluated immediately following the execution of an SRET instruction or an explicit write to a CSR on which these interrupt trap conditions expressly depend (including sip, sie and sstatus).

tags.name: norm:s_interrupt_priority
tags.text: Interrupts to S-mode take priority over any interrupts to lower privilege modes.

tags.name: norm:sip_acc
tags.text:
Each individual bit in register sip may be writable or may be
read-only.

tags.name: norm:sip_op
tags.text:
When bit i in sip is writable, a pending interrupt i
can be cleared by writing 0 to this bit. If interrupt i can become
pending but bit i in sip is read-only, the implementation must
provide some other mechanism for clearing the pending interrupt (which
may involve a call to the execution environment).

tags.name: norm:sie_acc
tags.text:
A bit in sie must be writable if the corresponding interrupt can ever
become pending. Bits of sie that are not writable are read-only zero.

tags.name: norm:sip_sie_bits_sz
tags.text: The standard portions (bits 15:0) of registers sip and sie are formatted as shown in Figures  and  respectively.

tags.name: norm:sip-seip_sie-seie
tags.text: Bits sip.SEIP and sie.SEIE are the interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable bits for supervisor-level external interrupts. If implemented, SEIP is read-only in sip, and is set and cleared by the execution environment, typically through a platform-specific interrupt controller.

tags.name: norm:sip-stip_sie-stie
tags.text: Bits sip.STIP and sie.STIE are the interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable bits for supervisor-level timer interrupts. If implemented, STIP is read-only in sip, and is set and cleared by the execution environment.

tags.text: Bits sip.STIP and sie.STIE are the interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable bits for supervisor-level timer interrupts. If implemented, STIP is read-only in sip, and is set and cleared by the execution environment.

tags.name: norm:sip-ssip_sie-ssie
tags.text: Bits sip.SSIP and sie.SSIE are the interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable bits for supervisor-level software interrupts. If implemented, SSIP is writable in sip and may also be set to 1 by a platform-specific interrupt controller.

tags.name: norm:sip-ssip_sie-ssie
tags.text: Bits sip.SSIP and sie.SSIE are the interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable bits for supervisor-level software interrupts. If implemented, SSIP is writable in sip and may also be set to 1 by a platform-specific interrupt controller.

tags.text: Bits sip.SSIP and sie.SSIE are the interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable bits for supervisor-level software interrupts. If implemented, SSIP is writable in sip and may also be set to 1 by a platform-specific interrupt controller.

tags.text: Bits sip.SSIP and sie.SSIE are the interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable bits for supervisor-level software interrupts. If implemented, SSIP is writable in sip and may also be set to 1 by a platform-specific interrupt controller.

tags.name: norm:sip_sie_Sscofpmf
tags.text: If the Sscofpmf extension is implemented, bits sip.LCOFIP and sie.LCOFIE are the interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable bits for local-counter-overflow interrupts. LCOFIP is read-write in sip and reflects the occurrence of a local counter-overflow overflow interrupt request resulting from any of the mhpmeventn.OF bits being set. If the Sscofpmf extension is not implemented, sip.LCOFIP and sie.LCOFIE are read-only zeros.

tags.name: norm:sip_sie_Sscofpmf
tags.text: If the Sscofpmf extension is implemented, bits sip.LCOFIP and sie.LCOFIE are the interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable bits for local-counter-overflow interrupts. LCOFIP is read-write in sip and reflects the occurrence of a local counter-overflow overflow interrupt request resulting from any of the mhpmeventn.OF bits being set. tags.name: norm:sip_sie_unimpl
tags.text: Each standard interrupt type (SEI, STI, SSI, or LCOFI) may not be implemented, in which case the corresponding interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable bits are read-only zeros. All bits in sip and sie are WARL fields. The implemented interrupts may be found by writing one to every bit location in sie, then reading back to see which bit positions hold a one.

tags.name: norm:sip_sie_unimpl
tags.text: Each standard interrupt type (SEI, STI, SSI, or LCOFI) may not be implemented, in which case the corresponding interrupt-pending and interrupt-enable bits are read-only zeros. All bits in sip and sie are WARL fields. The implemented interrupts may be found by writing one to every bit location in sie, then reading back to see which bit positions hold a one.

tags.name: norm:sip_sie_priority_bit_order
tags.text: Multiple simultaneous interrupts destined for supervisor mode are handled in the following decreasing priority order: SEI, SSI, STI, LCOFI.

tags.name: norm:sip_sie_priority_bit_order
tags.text: Multiple simultaneous interrupts destined for supervisor mode are handled in the following decreasing priority order: SEI, SSI, STI, LCOFI.

tags.name: norm:supervisor_timer_scheduling
tags.text: The implementation must provide a facility for scheduling timer interrupts in terms of the real-time counter, time.

tags.name: norm:scounteren
tags.text: The counter-enable (scounteren) CSR is a 32-bit register that controls the availability of the hardware performance monitoring counters to U-mode.

tags.name: norm:scounteren
tags.text: The counter-enable (scounteren) CSR is a 32-bit register that controls the availability of the hardware performance monitoring counters to U-mode.

tags.name: norm:scounteren_acc
tags.text: scounteren must be implemented. However, any of the bits may be read-only zero, indicating reads to the corresponding counter will cause an exception when executing in U-mode. Hence, they are effectively WARL fields.

tags.name: norm:scounteren_op
tags.text: When the CY, TM, IR, or HPMn bit in the scounteren register is clear, attempts to read the cycle, time, instret, or hpmcountern register while executing in U-mode will cause an illegal-instruction exception. When one of these bits is set, access to the corresponding register is permitted.

tags.name: norm:sscratch
tags.text: The sscratch CSR is an SXLEN-bit read/write register, dedicated for use by the supervisor. Typically, sscratch is used to hold a pointer to the hart-local supervisor context while the hart is executing user code. At the beginning of a trap handler, software normally uses a CSRRW instruction to swap sscratch with an integer register to obtain an initial working register.

At the beginning of a trap handler, software normally uses a CSRRW instruction to swap sscratch with an integer register to obtain an initial working register.

tags.name: norm:sepc
tags.text: sepc is an SXLEN-bit read/write CSR formatted as shown in . The low bit of sepc (sepc[0]) is always zero. On implementations that support only IALIGN=32, the two low bits (sepc[1:0]) are always zero.

tags.name: norm:sepc
tags.text: sepc is an SXLEN-bit read/write CSR formatted as shown in . The low bit of sepc (sepc[0]) is always zero. On implementations that support only IALIGN=32, the two low bits (sepc[1:0]) are always zero.

tags.name: norm:sepc
tags.text: sepc is an SXLEN-bit read/write CSR formatted as shown in . The low bit of sepc (sepc[0]) is always zero. On implementations that support only IALIGN=32, the two low bits (sepc[1:0]) are always zero.

tags.name: norm:sepc
tags.text: sepc is an SXLEN-bit read/write CSR formatted as shown in . The low bit of sepc (sepc[0]) is always zero. On implementations that support only IALIGN=32, the two low bits (sepc[1:0]) are always zero.

tags.name: norm:sepc_op_mask_ialign32
tags.text: If an implementation allows IALIGN to be either 16 or 32 (by changing CSR misa, for example), then, whenever IALIGN=32, bit sepc[1] is masked on reads so that it appears to be 0. This masking occurs also for the implicit read by the SRET instruction. Though masked, sepc[1] remains writable when IALIGN=32.

tags.name: norm:sepc_acc_invalid_addr
tags.text: sepc is a WARL register that must be able to hold all valid virtual addresses. It need not be capable of holding all possible invalid addresses. Prior to writing sepc, implementations may convert an invalid address into some other invalid address that sepc is capable of holding.

tags.name: norm:sepc_op_trap_write
tags.text: When a trap is taken into S-mode, sepc is written with the virtual address of the instruction that was interrupted or that encountered the exception. Otherwise, sepc is never written by the implementation, though it may be explicitly written by software.

tags.name: norm:scause
tags.text: The scause CSR is an SXLEN-bit read-write register formatted as shown in . When a trap is taken into S-mode, scause is written with a code indicating the event that caused the trap. Otherwise, scause is never written by the implementation, though it may be explicitly written by software.

tags.name: norm:scause
tags.text: The scause CSR is an SXLEN-bit read-write register formatted as shown in . When a trap is taken into S-mode, scause is written with a code indicating the event that caused the trap. Otherwise, scause is never written by the implementation, though it may be explicitly written by software.

tags.name: norm:scause
tags.text: The scause CSR is an SXLEN-bit read-write register formatted as shown in . When a trap is taken into S-mode, scause is written with a code indicating the event that caused the trap. Otherwise, scause is never written by the implementation, though it may be explicitly written by software.

tags.name: norm:scause-interrupt
tags.text: The Interrupt bit in the scause register is set if the trap was caused by an interrupt. The Exception Code field contains a code identifying the last exception or interrupt.  lists the possible exception codes for the current supervisor ISAs. The Exception Code is a WLRL field. It is required to hold the values 0–31 (i.e., bits 4–0 must be implemented), but otherwise it is only guaranteed to hold supported exception codes.

tags.name: norm:scause-interrupt
tags.text: The Interrupt bit in the scause register is set if the trap was caused by an interrupt. The Exception Code field contains a code identifying the last exception or interrupt.  lists the possible exception codes for the current supervisor ISAs. The Exception Code is a WLRL field. It is required to hold the values 0–31 (i.e., bits 4–0 must be implemented), but otherwise it is only guaranteed to hold supported exception codes.

tags.name: norm:scause-exception_code_acc
tags.text:
The
Exception Code is a WLRL field.

tags.name: norm:scause-exception_code_sz
tags.text:
It is required to hold the values 0–31
(i.e., bits 4–0 must be implemented), but otherwise it is only
guaranteed to hold supported exception codes.

tags.name: norm:stval
tags.text: The stval CSR is an SXLEN-bit read-write register formatted as shown in . When a trap is taken into S-mode, stval is written with exception-specific information to assist software in handling the trap. Otherwise, stval is never written by the implementation, though it may be explicitly written by software. The hardware platform will specify which exceptions must set stval informatively, which may unconditionally set it to zero, and which may exhibit either behavior, depending on the underlying event that caused the exception.
